items.myth: Conda is just an alternative to pip.
items.reality: Conda is both an environment manager and a package manager that handles non-Python binaries; pip only installs Python packages.
items.example: `conda install ffmpeg cuda-toolkit pytorch` installs three things: a C codec library, a GPU runtime, and a Python ML framework — all with matched versions and ABI. Pip can't do the first two.

items.myth: You can't use pip inside conda.
items.reality: You can — install conda packages first, then pip, ideally declaring pip deps in environment.yml; just avoid haphazard interleaving.
items.example:
```yaml
dependencies:
  - pytorch          # conda — needs the binary
  - pip
  - pip:
    - langchain     # pip — pure-Python, not on conda-forge
```

items.myth: Anaconda and conda are the same thing.
items.reality: conda is the tool; Anaconda is a large distribution bundling conda plus many packages. Miniconda is a minimal installer with just conda.
items.example: Disk cost: full Anaconda ≈ 3 GB (preloaded with ~250 packages). Miniconda ≈ 50 MB (just conda + Python). For CI / production, install Miniconda and add only what you need.
